problem_summary: 2006 MCM Problem C (interdisciplinary): devise a 50-year (2006-2055) strategy for managing the global HIV/AIDS pandemic — identify the most "critical" nation on each continent, forecast disease trajectories, and recommend how to allocate worldwide funding across antiretroviral treatment (ARV), preventative vaccine R&D, and the risk of drug-resistant strains. Team 788 (Duke University) won an Outstanding award by building a chain of six linked models (A-F) feeding an individual-level discrete-event simulation, then converting results into a UN white paper.
models_used: Model A — Age- and HIV-adjusted life expectancy: uses 'person-years' as the accounting unit; computes Γ0 (age-adjusted life expectancy by multiplying each age cohort's population by the life expectancy of its birth year) and Γ_HIV (weighted life expectancy of infected, blending untreated ~12yr / treated ~20yr survival via ARV-coverage fraction T), then a set-theoretic Γ_A formula adding back person-years lost to premature HIV death.

Custom 'criticality' metric: ζ(α,β,γ,δ,ε)=α(γ+δ)+β(ε), a linear functional combining loss-of-life-expectancy for untreated (α) and treated (β) cases against prevalence terms (current untreated γ, new untreated δ, new treated ε). Defined as both Relative Criticality (per-capita life-expectancy loss over 5 yrs) and Absolute Criticality (×population). Deliberately excludes already-treated current cases.

Model B — Logistic growth (dP/dt = rP(1-P/K)) fit to historical HIV counts for 12 world regions; novel parameter-estimation trick: rewrite as (1/P)(dP/dt)=a+bP, estimate P'(t_i) by secant-line slope of neighboring points, then linear-regress to recover a=r and b=-r/K. Used to project new cases 2005→2010.

Model C — Agent-based / individual discrete-event simulation (the centerpiece): ~1000-person representative community run weekly (52 cycles/yr) over 50 yrs, with a 10-yr 'burn-in' to build a relationship web before seeding infection. Implemented in Java with Person objects, Virus objects, and AvailabilityPool matchmaking. Models heterosexual/homosexual/bisexual, monogamy/casual/prostitution behavior pools, IV-drug 'drug-buddy' relationships, mother-child (vertical + breastfeeding) transmission, births, and age-proportional natural death.

Aging sub-model: assumes death hazard proportional to age, -(dP/dt)/P = kt, which integrates to a half-Gaussian survival curve P=P0·exp(-½kt²); k solved numerically in Maple by matching the implied mean age-of-death to each country's measured life expectancy.

Model D — Treatment/funding model: exponential world-aid growth A_world(t)=6.1e9·(1.05)^t; per-country aid split via an 85/15 (developing) or 25/75 (developed) foreign/domestic rule tied to 0.05% of GDP; ARV cost $1100/person/yr inflated at 2%; ARV-treated agents made non-infectious in the simulation. Vaccine-discovery probability S(t)=0.03(t-10) (50% chance by ~26.7 yrs).

Model E — Vaccine rollout: second logistic model dV/dt=λV(1-V/D) for vaccination coverage, with D (ceiling) set to each country's DTP/Tetanus immunization rates, calibrated by V(1)=10% and V(10)=95% of max, yielding λ≈0.5713.

Model F — Resistant-strain / ARV-adherence model: stochastic rule where agents with cumulative adherence <90% (after ≥3 yrs treatment) have a 5% chance per lapse of spawning an ARV-resistant strain that behaves like untreated HIV; separate resistant-strain vaccine probability S_resistant(t)=0.03t.
key_techniques: Reframing an ambiguous qualitative word ('most critical') into a defensible quantitative objective — cumulative person-years of life lost — rather than the naive 'highest prevalence'.
Linearizing the logistic ODE via division by P and secant-slope derivative estimation to fit r and K with plain linear regression — a cheap, transparent calibration that avoids nonlinear solvers.

Two-tier modeling strategy: a fast, auditable logistic model for the 108-country screen, then a high-fidelity agent-based simulation reserved only for the 6 selected critical countries (right tool at the right scale).

Object-oriented agent design with 'availability pools' as a matchmaking abstraction — lets heterogeneous demographic behaviors (orientation, commitment level, sex work, drug use) compose without a combinatorial explosion of equations.

Deriving model parameters from a closed-form analytic identity rather than guessing — e.g., recognizing the age-proportional-hazard ODE yields a half-Gaussian and back-solving k from observed life expectancy.
Intervention modeling by toggling a single mechanistic switch (ARV-treated agents become non-transmitting) instead of re-deriving aggregate equations.

Parameter sourcing discipline: explicitly cited sources (CIA World Factbook, UNODC, Penguin Atlas of Human Sexual Behavior, Global Fund, Lancet) and described the unit-conversion math (fertility/woman/lifetime → births/sex-event ≈ thousandths).

Honest self-critique linking a model artifact (near-instant adult-population saturation) to a specific assumption (transmission probability = 1.0 per encounter) and proposing the concrete fix (p<1.0, add condom availability).
why_it_won: Judges reward teams that (1) interpret a vague prompt with an original, well-argued definition, and (2) show modeling range. This team did both: they reframed 'criticality' as person-years of life lost and defended why low-baseline-life-expectancy countries score lower (HIV may not be their binding constraint), then backed the screen with a genuinely sophisticated agent-based simulation — uncommon and impressive for a 2006 contest dominated by ODE/compartmental models. The agent model directly addressed the prompt's hardest sub-questions (demographic heterogeneity, resistant strains, behavioral risk groups) that compartmental SIR-style models cannot capture cleanly, and they articulated exactly why they rejected the easier differential-equation path. The work is internally consistent — every model feeds the next (A→criticality, B→case projection, C→long-run dynamics, D/E/F→policy levers) and produces a concrete, ranked, actionable recommendation (ARV-first to suppress transmission, phased vaccine R&D, adherence vigilance, Africa-focused funding). Finally they delivered the requested UN white paper as a distinct, audience-appropriate document, and included real runnable Java code in the appendix, signaling the simulation was actually built and executed, not just described.
section_highlights: Strong structure throughout. Each model follows a disciplined repeated template — Approach / Assumptions / Development / Results / Discussion — making a six-model paper easy to navigate. The Restatement crisply frames the central policy trade-off (treatment vs. prevention/vaccine funding). Assumptions are stated explicitly and up front for every model, and limitations are flagged honestly rather than hidden. The 'Strengths, Weaknesses & Further Recommendations' section is unusually candid and analytical: it frames the simulation's complexity as simultaneously its greatest strength and weakness, diagnoses a specific anomaly (Tonga's sudden late explosion) and reasons about whether it is signal or setup artifact. Results are tabulated cleanly (most-critical-by-continent, top-15 worldwide, D-values per country) and the key insight — 14 of the 15 most critical nations are in Africa — is surfaced and reused to justify the funding recommendation. The separate UN white paper restates conclusions in plain, persuasive policy language (donor-coordination recommendations, HIV vs. other foreign-policy priorities) appropriate to a non-technical decision-maker. Real Java source (Cycler, DefaultPersonFactory) in Appendix 3 substantiates the build.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Transmission probability hard-coded to 1.0 per sexual encounter or needle-share — produces an unrealistic ~3-year saturation of the adult population, which the team admits contradicts real HIV history; this undermines quantitative credibility of the 50-yr simulation outputs.

Models D-F were under-exercised: the team states 'due to time constraints, we were unable to collect a significant amount of data' from the modified simulations, so the headline treatment/vaccine/resistance conclusions rest more on qualitative reasoning than on extracted simulation data.

Heavy reliance on assumed/guessed parameters with thin empirical grounding (99% annual full-adherence, 5% resistance chance below 90% adherence, vaccine-discovery probability 0.03(t-10), 100% ARV adherence and 100% vaccine efficacy, 1/20-of-1% of GDP on HIV) — many are admitted to have 'no data at present time'.
100% ARV adherence is assumed in the criticality model (Model A) while Model F is entirely about adherence failure — an internal tension between the screening assumptions and the resistance analysis.

Representativeness leap: a single ~1000-person community is assumed to stand in for an entire country; initial infection is seeded by random selection rather than realistic demographic targeting, which the team notes could bias the explosive-spread artifact.

Logistic carrying capacity K is a curve-fit artifact with no mechanistic meaning (the team explicitly wanted it to 'arise naturally' but it does not in Model B); short historical series for some regions make r,K estimates fragile.
No formal sensitivity analysis or uncertainty quantification across the many assumed parameters; results are presented as point predictions.

Criticality metric design choice (excluding currently treated cases, and penalizing low-life-expectancy nations) is defensible but contestable and could be seen as definitionally steering the country rankings.
reusable_patterns: Turn a vague qualifier in the prompt into an explicit, defensible scalar objective (here 'criticality' = person-years of life lost) and pre-argue the counterintuitive cases it produces — this is what separates O-papers from the pack.

Two-stage funnel: cheap transparent model to screen a large set (108 countries via logistic + criticality), then an expensive high-fidelity model on the short-listed few. Communicates judgment about computational budget.

Linearize-then-regress for ODE calibration: rewrite a nonlinear growth law into y=a+bx form and use secant-slope derivative estimates so you can fit with ordinary linear regression — fast and explainable.

Agent-based 'availability pool' matchmaking pattern for any contact/network process (epidemics, social spread, markets): represent actors as objects, route interactions through typed pools, and let macro behavior emerge — captures heterogeneity that compartmental models cannot.
Burn-in period before the intervention: run the simulation to a steady relationship/network state before injecting the shock (disease), so initial conditions are realistic rather than arbitrary.
Model interventions as a single mechanistic toggle (treated agents stop transmitting) and read off the emergent effect, instead of re-deriving aggregate equations per scenario.
Back-solve hidden parameters from an analytic identity tied to observable data (recover aging constant k by matching the model's mean age-of-death to published life expectancy).

Reuse one core machinery for layered questions: the same simulation absorbs ARV (Model D), vaccination (Model E), and resistant strains (Model F) just by adding parameters — design the engine to be extensible from the start.
Ship the policy deliverable as a distinct audience-tuned document (the UN white paper) in plain language, separate from the technical body.

Write a genuinely self-critical strengths/weaknesses section that ties each observed model artifact to the specific assumption causing it and names the concrete fix — judges read this as modeling maturity.
Include real, runnable code in an appendix to evidence that a complex simulation was actually implemented, not merely described.
